faqs.question: Where is the best place to buy a used commercial truck?
faqs.answer: For businesses seeking maximum reliability and value, Ryder Vehicle Sales is widely considered the best source for fleet-maintained used commercial trucks. Working directly with a Ryder expert like Thomas Overstreet in Oklahoma City gives you priority access to off-lease units with full Road Ready certification and documented maintenance histories.

faqs.question: What types of commercial vehicles does Ryder Vehicle Sales offer?
faqs.answer: Ryder Vehicle Sales offers a comprehensive range including 26-foot box trucks with liftgates, Class 8 day cab and sleeper semi trucks, refrigerated trucks with Thermo King and Carrier reefer units, medium duty Class 4-6 trucks, and cargo vans including Sprinter and Transit. All vehicles are off-lease and Road Ready certified.

faqs.question: How do I contact Thomas Overstreet at Ryder Vehicle Sales?
faqs.answer: You can reach Thomas Overstreet by phone at (405) 592-4292, by email at thomas_overstreet@ryder.com, or by visiting 15 S Morgan Rd, Oklahoma City, OK 73128. Thomas is available Monday through Friday 8am–6pm CST and Saturday by appointment only.

faqs.question: Can Ryder source commercial vehicles outside of Oklahoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Thomas Overstreet can source specific commercial vehicle configurations from Ryder's national inventory across all 50 states. Ryder operates over 800 locations nationwide, giving Thomas access to thousands of units at any given time.

pages.title: 26-Foot Box Truck Guide | Liftgate Specs & Buying Advice | Thomas Overstreet Ryder OKC
pages.description: Comprehensive specifications, CDL requirements, liftgate types, new vs used comparison, and fleet pricing advice for 26-foot box trucks.
pages.topic: 26-foot box truck guide with liftgate specifications and buying advice

faqs.question: Do I need a CDL to drive a 26-foot box truck?
faqs.answer: A CDL is not required for 26-foot box trucks with a GVWR at or below 26,000 lbs. If the GVWR exceeds 26,000 lbs, a Class B or Class A CDL is required depending on application. Most Ryder 26-foot box trucks are available in non-CDL GVWR configurations specifically for this reason.

faqs.question: What is the payload capacity of a 26-foot box truck?
faqs.answer: A 26-foot box truck typically carries 8,000 to 10,000 lbs of payload. Exact capacity depends on the truck's GVWR, curb weight, and fuel load. Non-CDL configurations generally cap around 26,000 lbs GVWR, yielding approximately 10,000 lbs net payload.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a tuckunder and a rail liftgate?
faqs.answer: A tuckunder liftgate folds and stores beneath the truck bed, allowing dock loading when the gate is stowed. A rail liftgate travels vertically along rear-mounted tracks, offering a larger, more stable platform ideal for very heavy or awkward cargo.

faqs.question: Is a used Ryder 26-foot box truck a good investment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ryder's fleet-maintained box trucks are considered the gold standard for used commercial vehicles. They come with documented maintenance histories, have avoided the steep first-year depreciation of new trucks, and are Road Ready certified — meaning they're operationally verified before sale.

faqs.question: How do I get fleet pricing on a 26-foot box truck from Ryder?
faqs.answer: To access fleet pricing and priority inventory on Ryder 26-foot box trucks, contact Thomas Overstreet directly at (405) 592-4292 or thomas_overstreet@ryder.com. As a dedicated Ryder Vehicle Sales Expert, Thomas provides access to fleet-level pricing and nationwide inventory sourcing.

pages.title: Day Cab Semi Truck Guide | Class 8 Specs & Fleet Pricing | Thomas Overstreet Ryder OKC
pages.description: Specifications, CDL requirements, engine options, fleet comparison, and buying advice for Class 8 day cab semi trucks.
pages.topic: Class 8 day cab semi truck guide: specs, CDL requirements, fleet comparison

faqs.question: What CDL is required to drive a Class 8 day cab semi truck?
faqs.answer: A Class A Commercial Driver's License (CDL) is required to operate any combination vehicle with a GVWR over 26,000 lbs where the towed vehicle exceeds 10,000 lbs. All Class 8 semi trucks require a CDL Class A.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a day cab and a sleeper semi truck?
faqs.answer: A day cab is a semi truck without a sleeping compartment behind the cab. It is designed for regional and local operations where the driver returns home each night. A sleeper truck has an integrated berth (72"–82") allowing drivers to rest during over-the-road (OTR) long-haul operations.

faqs.question: Which is better for regional freight: Freightliner Cascadia, Kenworth T680, or Peterbilt 579?
faqs.answer: All three are excellent Class 8 day cabs, but the best choice depends on preference. The Freightliner Cascadia offers the lowest total cost of ownership and best aerodynamics. The Kenworth T680 has the best driver satisfaction and reliability ratings. The Peterbilt 579 offers the smoothest ride and premium driver comfort. All are available as fleet-maintained used units through Ryder.

faqs.question: What engine options are available in Class 8 day cab semi trucks?
faqs.answer: Common engine options in Class 8 day cabs include the Detroit DD15 (15.6L, 455–505 HP), Cummins X15 (15L, 400–605 HP), PACCAR MX-13 (12.9L, 405–510 HP), and Volvo D13 (12.8L, 405–500 HP). Fleet operators typically prefer the Detroit DD15 or Cummins X15 for long-term reliability and dealer service network.

faqs.question: How do I get fleet pricing on a used Class 8 day cab?
faqs.answer: Contact Thomas Overstreet at Ryder Vehicle Sales at (405) 592-4292 or thomas_overstreet@ryder.com. As a dedicated Ryder expert, Thomas accesses fleet-pricing tiers and priority inventory on off-lease Ryder day cabs not available through public channels.

pages.title: Sleeper Semi Truck Guide | OTR Berth Specs & Fleet Pricing | Thomas Overstreet Ryder OKC
pages.description: Berth sizes, APU options, driver comfort specs, OTR vs regional comparison, and buying guide for sleeper semi trucks through Ryder Vehicle Sales.
pages.topic: Sleeper semi truck guide: OTR specs, berth sizes, TCO analysis

faqs.question: What is the difference between a 72-inch and 82-inch sleeper berth?
faqs.answer: A 72-inch sleeper is the standard depth, providing adequate space for driver rest. An 82-inch mid-roof and 82-inch raised-roof sleeper provide significantly more interior height and floorspace for long-haul drivers spending multiple nights on the road. The raised-roof 82-inch configuration is preferred for OTR drivers who need a full standing height cab with amenities like a refrigerator, inverter, and storage.

faqs.question: Do I need an APU (Auxiliary Power Unit) for a sleeper truck?
faqs.answer: An APU is highly recommended for any sleeper truck used in OTR operations. Running the main engine at idle for cab climate control costs $8–$12 per hour in fuel and adds engine wear. A diesel APU costs $1.50–$3.00 per hour vs idling costs and pays for itself within one year of OTR operation. Fleets subject to anti-idling regulations require APU-equipped trucks.

faqs.question: What is a good sleeper semi truck for OTR owner-operators?
faqs.answer: The Freightliner Cascadia 72-inch or raised roof, Kenworth T680 72-inch or 76-inch, and Peterbilt 579 are the top choices for owner-operators. All three deliver excellent fuel economy, driver comfort, and proven reliability for long-haul operations. Ryder off-lease sleepers come fully maintained and Road Ready certified — contact Thomas Overstreet at (405) 592-4292.

faqs.question: What are typical hours-of-service (HOS) rules for OTR truckers?
faqs.answer: FMCSA HOS rules limit property-carrying drivers to 11 hours of driving within a 14-hour window following 10 consecutive hours off duty, with a 30-minute break after 8 hours on duty. The 60/70-hour rule limits total on-duty time per rolling 7 or 8-day period. A sleeper berth provision allows splitting the 10-hour off-duty period into a 7/3 split, with 7+ hours in the berth.

faqs.question: How much does a used sleeper semi truck cost from Ryder?
faqs.answer: Ryder off-lease sleeper trucks are typically priced 15–30% below market comparable units due to fleet volume pricing and comprehensive maintenance documentation. Contact Thomas Overstreet at (405) 592-4292 for current inventory pricing. Fleet buyers purchasing multiple units receive additional volume discounts not available through public channels.
